"A city ordinance providing for


the punishment of every. person
who shall, on any street, sidewalk
or alley, or public place, or in or


upon any private house, building or
premises, act in noisy, riotous or
disorderly manner, mereyI uses the
term 'riotous' in its popular mean
ing Of wanton and boisterous, and


has no allusion to the technical


crime of riot"


This decision, handed down by
Supreme Court Judge c. J. Revis,
with Anders, Dunbar and White
concurring. is the one on which
Judge S. A. Mann of the police
court is basing his decisions in the
disorderly conduct cases, brought
by the city against street speakers
1t is a decision rendered to II. l..
KEENAN,, sustaining conviction on
disorderly conduct under the pres.
, ent Spokane city ordinance, by
Judge w. l.. Belt


Thus, II is seen, that so long as
the street speaking cases are
brought to court under the disor'
derby conduct charges, Judge Mann


can scarcely be unseated in his
judgment, finding the defendants
guilty, despite the fact that he has
practically ruled that free speech


is constitutional right.


City officials openly admit that


they could scarcely hope to convict


and punish the street speakers un
der the old ordinance that appears


to Illegally prohibit the practice OF
street speaking.
